exemplar.title: To what extent is there evidence of an urban microclimate in Panama City, Panama?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is there evidence of an urban microclimate in Panama City, Panama?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is concise, explicitly geographical and linked to urban micro-climate theory
Geographic context is precise with clearly identified sites and justification of feasibility
Strong alignment to the IB syllabus and clear hypothesis operationalisation
weaknesses: Map labels are small and one route map is cluttered by photographs, reducing readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands fully satisfy the highest-band descriptors (question formulation and location specificity) and mapping conventions are largely followed with only minor issues. The overall presentation demonstrates the qualities expected in band 3, warranting the full 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Sampling strategy, timing and variable selection are thoroughly explained and linked to theory
Instrumentation and procedures are clearly documented with photographic support
weaknesses: Green-space estimation is subjective and could be improved with GIS or photographic analysis
Interval selection criteria for site spacing are not fully specified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method description fully meets top-band requirements, but data-collection techniques show some subjectivity and limited site sampling. This performance aligns with the middle band, meriting 2 of 3 marks.

strengths: All data collected are directly relevant to testing the hypotheses
Appropriate use of bar/line graphs, maps and statistical tables
Figures are numbered and most follow labelling conventions
weaknesses: Occasional errors in conventions (misspellings, small captions)
Could use more advanced presentation (e.g., GIS outputs, error bars)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance is strong, and presentation techniques are appropriate. Minor lapses in conventions keep the work within the 3â4 band. Given the upper-end performance, 4 of 6 marks are justified.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Correct application of descriptive and statistical techniques (Spearmanâs rank)
Clear linking of patterns and anomalies to hypotheses and theory
Thoughtful interpretation of anomalies in context of urban climate processes
weaknesses: Analysis would benefit from additional statistical tests (p-values, confidence intervals)
Cross-variable relationships (e.g., temperature vs green space) are not fully tested
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student employs appropriate analytical and interpretative techniques and links findings to theory, placing performance clearly in the 3â4 band. Some gaps exist, but they are not so substantial as to limit the mark to the lower band. Awarding 4 of 8 reflects the strength of the analysis within the middle band.

strengths: Conclusion revisits each hypothesis and is explicitly supported by the analysis
Acknowledges contradictions and provides clear evidence-based closure
weaknesses: Could more explicitly link conclusions back to UHI theory and methodological choices
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear and fully supported by the findings, satisfying all descriptors for band 2. Thus the full 2 marks for this criterion are awarded.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant strengths and weaknesses with explanation of their effects on reliability and validity
Improvement suggestions (e.g., multi-season sampling, night-time measurements, GIS NDVI analysis) are realistic and tied to accuracy enhancements
Demonstrates awareness of feasibility and impact of proposed changes
weaknesses: Suggestions could include more detail on resource requirements and implementation protocols
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strengths and weaknesses are comprehensively explained and the suggested improvements are detailed with clear links to their potential impact. This meets the highest-band descriptors for band 3, justifying the full 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Three maps are provided; all carry scale bars and north arrows, but figure labelling is occasionally small and one map (route map) is cluttered by photographs, making distances hard to read.  Mapping conventions are generally followed yet not consistently clear, placing the work in the âappropriate but with minor issuesâ band rather than the highest.

The fieldwork question is clearly phrased, overtly geographical and explicitly tied to the Urban Environments optional theme (urban micro-climates/UHI).  The wording allows the student to generate three explicit hypotheses, demonstrating that the question can be operationalised and tested.  Consequently, the descriptors for the top band are met.

The student employs bar/line graphs, maps, photos and a Spearman calculationâappropriate and generally effective.  However, more sophisticated multivariate or GIS outputs would have strengthened communication, so the presentation is judged âappropriateâ rather than âmost effectiveâ.

Instrumentation and step-by-step photographs show techniques were correctly applied, and two runs were completed; however, only seven sites were used and the green-space estimate relied on visual judgement, introducing notable subjectivity.  Data quantity is adequate but not âhigh-qualityâ throughout, fitting the mid-band.

Descriptive statistics, a Spearmanâs rank test and comparative graphs are used correctly, demonstrating suitable analytical technique beyond simple description.  Confidence levels or multiple tests are absent, so the work does not reach the 5-6 band but satisfies the 3-4 band at its upper end.

Patterns and anomalies are discussed and linked to the hypotheses and urban climate theory (albedo, evapotranspiration, sea breezes).  The reasoning is coherent but not exhaustive; some explanations remain speculative.  This meets the descriptor for band 3-4, mid-range.

The student identifies Panama City and pinpoints the PLVI (F&F Tower) as the central reference.  Distances for each of the seven sites are stated and justified, showing the investigation is feasible with primary data.  Climatic context (dry season, typical temperatures) is included, fully satisfying the requirement for precise, workable location information.

Strengths (instrument precision, sample size) and weaknesses (seasonality, subjectivity) are outlined with brief explanation of their effect on reliability and validity.  The treatment is appropriate but not fully comprehensive, aligning with band 2.

All collected variables (temperature, cloud, traffic, green space, building height) are directly relevant to testing the hypotheses, and 56 data sets provide a reasonable basis for analysis.  Nevertheless, only seven spatial points limit robustness, so the work sits at the upper end of the 3-4 band rather than the 5-6 band.

A clear concluding section revisits each hypothesis and is explicitly supported by summarized findings, even acknowledging contradictions.  Although the support stems mainly from descriptive evidence rather than rigorous statistics, the requirements for full support are met.

Analysis covers each hypothesis but leaves gapsâe.g., no cross-variable statistical testing for temperature and green space, limited temporal analysis, and only brief treatment of building height.  These omissions indicate âsome gapsâ, fitting the 1-2 band, upper mark.

Sampling strategy, timing, and variable selection are thoroughly explained with justification linking each variable to a hypothesis and relevant theory (UHI, condensation nuclei, evapotranspiration).  The description is detailed enough for replication and clearly aligned with the research question, matching the top-band descriptor.

Most graphs have titles, labelled axes and units; figures are numbered and sourced.  Minor lapses (e.g., misspelled âokras/oktasâ, small captions, some unlabeled photo scales) show occasional convention errors, placing the work within the mid-band.

The student proposes relevant improvementsâadditional sites, multi-season sampling, night-time measurements, and software for green-space analysisâand briefly explains how each would enhance accuracy.  While sensible, the discussion of impact lacks depth, so the work fits comfortably in band 2 rather than 3.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question is concise, explicitly geographical and linked to urban microclimate theory, providing a strong foundation for hypothesis development.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Maps include scale bars and north arrows but labels are small and one route map is cluttered by photos, reducing readability of distances.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographical context is clear, but including precise coordinates or a grid reference table would enhance location specificity and facilitate replication.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to the IB Geography syllabus and identification of the Urban Environments option demonstrates clear alignment with course requirements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Three explicit hypotheses are well formulated with theoretical justification, allowing clear operationalisation and testing.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure caption lacks a full reference; include complete source citation (author, year, URL) consistently in bibliography.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The method for determining PLVI is clearly explained with justification linking realâestate pricing to hypothesis testing, ensuring replicability.
Sampling strategy is systematic but interval selection is unclear; specify distances or criteria for site spacing to reduce selection bias.
Justification of measuring nearâsurface temperature, air temperature and building height demonstrates clear theoretical alignment with UHI processes.
Visually estimating green space percentage introduces subjectivity; use photographic analysis software or GIS NDVI methods to improve precision.
Provide detailed protocol for cloud okta and traffic counts (observer calibration, duration of counts) to ensure consistency across sites.
Figure 11 is effective in combining variables but lacks clear axis labels and units; ensure all graphs follow labelling conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The anomaly at Site 5 is thoughtfully interpreted with reference to green space and material albedo, showing strong linking to urban climate theory.
The analysis refers to patterns descriptively but would benefit from additional statistical tests to support claims beyond descriptive trends.
Conclusion succinctly summarizes whether hypotheses were supported and acknowledges contradictions, providing clear evidence-based closure.
Graph of green space could be improved by adding axis labels, units and error bars to convey variability and strengthen interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The full Spearman table spans n=4â30 but only n=7 is needed; condense the table to the relevant critical value to improve clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Include stepâbyâstep Spearmanâs rank formula and working in the report or appendix to improve transparency and replicability.
Correct interpretation of râ and null hypothesis demonstrates proper use of statistical testing in context of spatial correlation.
Discussion notes nonâsignificance but would benefit from reporting p-values or confidence intervals to strengthen statistical interpretation.
Figures 16 and 17 effectively use line graphs to illustrate diurnal patterns in cloud and traffic data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Analysis clearly links traffic trends to cloud cover observations, demonstrating coherent integration of data and theory.
Conclusion summarises findings but would be stronger if explicitly linked back to underlying UHI theory and methodological decisions.
Evaluation highlights instrument precision and sample size effectively, demonstrating awareness of reliability strengths.
Improvement suggestions are relevant but would benefit from details on feasibility, required resources and expected impact.
Recommend using GIS software (e.g., QGIS NDVI analysis) to quantify green space objectively, improving data accuracy.
